item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I may have missed something, but what people still have memories of the old reality? And why them? Also, still untitled!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The author may not have set the name yet.

The title isn't tied to the article directly. The author has to edit it in to the mainlist.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Is it just me or is the mainlist title still saying [ACCESS DENIED]

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
What's really hurting this for me is that the actual changes due to the CK are really undersold, and pretty vague. This has the affect of taking a lot of the impact out of the memo; it simply doesn't have the punch it could have if the alteration were more apparent.

The change isn't quite as stark as were made to beleive it is. From the article itself, were really only shown that the kaiju attack itself and its direct consequences are what's changed, so I'm left wondering how far-reaching the CK actually is, and why it's porgrayed in the memo as something systemic across all of their reality. Why would reverting an event that only just occured erase people and events occuring long before it?

There's also no real line delineating who or what is left over in this new world, so this doesn't help to invest one in the idea. It's not all Foundation personnel, apparently, because the operative in the log is replaced, and it's seems a touch inconsistant since they've still got the kaiju bits on-hand.

The last two addenda seem like they're supposed to be an aha moment, but it doesnt seem to be really hinted at beforehand or elaborated upon after the fact, so with the rest of the document not giving enough to support what seems to have been intended as a reveal, it's just going over my head.

ETA: I would consider taking a look at Wrong Proposal, which may just be the best-executed 'post-reality-restructuring ' we have on-site, at least, as far as showcasing the fallout of such a situation is concerned.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
There's issues, but in my opinion, not everything has to be perfect for something to be good.

This is good. Maybe not great but good enough.

I think at the end of the day, it's the formatting which is the weakest part of this, more than the concept or execution.

Some of the addenda, or reveals should have been more…revealed, I guess? Either way, +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like it, but I feel that it could be better. +1 for the competent execution

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I liked the article but the final two addendum in my opinion were unneeded +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's definitely rough around the edges, but I liked it.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's not bad… but it's not great. I've seen the 'sobering implications of a restructuring or reset' plotline in a few articles- SCP-2000 started the trend, I think. The last month or so, Kaiju have been all the rage, as well. Maybe it's just because I'm inured to these elements, but this doesn't grab me.

But, then, it was competently executed. No vote for now. I'll come back and read this again in a bit, and try to decide if I like it or not.

EDIT: Read it again. Decided it was pretty good, especially for a new author, and my motives for not upvoting were hipsterish and invalid. So, +1.
